title: The Autonomic Ladder and Why You Keep Falling Off It
researcherTopic: polyvagal theory, neuroception, the social engagement system
metaDescription: The autonomic ladder isn't for climbing. Learn why healing trauma means befriending the fall and how Polyvagal Theory helps you navigate your nervous system.
imageDescription: A soft, golden light illuminates a delicate, branching structure reminiscent of a neural network or a tree's root system. The light originates from a central point and flows outwards, suggesting a gentle, life-giving energy. The background is warm and out of focus, creating a feeling of peace and possibility.
htmlContent:
Nobody tells you that the goal isn’t to climb the autonomic ladder and stay at the top. The real work is learning how to fall with grace, and understanding the architecture of your own descent.

The Illusion of Control on the Autonomic Ladder

We are given this metaphor - the autonomic ladder, a concept born from the brilliant work of Deb Dana who translated Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory into a map we can supposedly work through. We are told there are three rungs: the grounded, connected state of ventral vagal at the top; the mobilized, anxious state of sympathetic in the middle; and the collapsed, shut-down state of dorsal vagal at the bottom. The implicit promise is that with enough effort, enough awareness, enough of the right kind of breathing, we can ascend to that top rung and build a permanent home there. The ladder is not a hierarchy of achievement; it is a description of response. To treat it as a game of ascent is to remain trapped in the logic of the thinking mind, a mind that believes it can will the nervous system into submission.

We think we are in control, that we are the climbers, but we are not. We are the ladder itself, and the climbing is happening to us, through us, orchestrated by a much older and wiser intelligence. The attempt to consciously manage our state, to force ourselves up the rungs, often just adds another layer of tension, another subtle signal of threat to a system that is already on high alert. The real work is not in the climbing, but in cultivating the conditions for the bloom to happen on its own. It is a shift from control to cultivation, from management to deep listening. What if the ladder isn't something to be conquered, but something to be understood, something to be inhabited with a radical sense of permission?

Neuroception: The Unseen Architect of Your State

The architect of our position on this ladder is not our willpower, but a process Stephen Porges termed neuroception. It is the silent, sub-cortical listening of the nervous system, constantly scanning the environment - internal and external... for cues of safety and danger. It turns out to be happening far below the threshold of conscious thought, in the ancient structures of the brainstem. Your neuroceptive circuits are taking in the prosody of a voice, the subtle shifts in facial muscles, the quality of light in the room, the tension in your own gut, and making a constant, instantaneous judgment: Safe enough to connect? You don’t decide to move up or down the ladder; your neuroception makes the choice for you, based on a lifetime of stored data about what constitutes a threat. In my years of working in this territory, I've seen countless people try to argue with their own neuroception, to convince themselves they are safe when their body is screaming otherwise. It never works. The body’s wisdom is older than the thinking mind’s logic.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link).

Ascending and Descending: The Rhythms of Regulation and Dysregulation

The Myth of the Top Rung: Why Safety is a Verb, Not a Destination

One of the most pervasive myths in healing is the idea that safety is a destination you can arrive at, a permanent state you can achieve. This myth is what keeps us striving, what keeps us climbing, what keeps us feeling like failures when we inevitably "fall off the ladder." But safety, from a polyvagal perspective, is not a place. It is a state of being that is co-created in relationship. It is the felt sense that arises when our neuroception detects cues of safety in another nervous system. We are not meant to do this alone. A client once described this as feeling like her nervous system was an orchestra without a conductor until she was in the presence of someone truly regulated. The idea that we can, or should, be able to achieve a state of perpetual self-regulation is a trauma response in itself... a byproduct of having to survive in environments where co-regulation was not available.

That becomes why a link to a resource like the International Society for the Study of Trauma and Dissociation can be so vital, as it points toward the relational nature of healing. The work is not to become an unshakable fortress of self-regulation. The work is to become skilled at finding and creating moments of co-regulation, both with others and within our own internal system. It is to learn how to offer ourselves the same cues of safety that we would offer to a frightened child. It is a dance, not a destination. And like any dance, it involves movement, rhythm, and the willingness to sometimes lose your footing. The process often involves understanding structural dissociation and how it impacts our capacity for connection.

Befriending the Fall: What to Do When You Lose Your Footing

So what does it mean to befriend the fall? It means shifting your attention from the goal of getting back to the top of the ladder to the practice of being present with wherever you are on it. It means that when you feel the familiar surge of anxiety or the heavy pull of shutdown, your first move is not to fix it, but to notice it. What emerges is the body mobilizing to face a perceived threat." Or, "This is the dorsal vagal system taking me offline to conserve energy." This simple act of naming, of acknowledging the wisdom of the body’s response, is itself a cue of safety. It sends a message to the deeper parts of the brain that you are not at war with your own experience.

From this place of noticing, we can introduce small, gentle anchors to the present moment. That becomes not about forcing a state change, but about offering an invitation. It might be the feeling of your feet on the floor, the texture of the fabric of your chair, the sound of a bird outside the window. There is a part of you that is not caught in the storm, but is the sky through which the storm is passing. It is a practice of dual awareness ~ holding the truth of the dysregulation and the truth of the present moment simultaneously. This is the essence of what it means to build a life of conscious awareness and healing.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link).

What Changes From Here

Ultimately, the work of healing complex trauma is not about eliminating the triggers or permanently residing at the top of the autonomic ladder. It is about changing our fundamental relationship to our own nervous system. It is a process of becoming an intimate student of our own biology, of learning to read the subtle signals of our body not as problems to be solved, but as communications to be received with curiosity and compassion. It is about understanding that every fall down the ladder is an opportunity to practice the return, to strengthen the neural pathways of resilience, to deepen the trust in our own capacity to move through the inevitable storms of life.

This path transforms the ladder from a hierarchy of success and failure into a map of your own inner space. Each rung becomes a familiar territory, a place you have learned to inhabit with awareness. The goal is no longer to escape the lower rungs, but to learn how to bring the qualities of the top rung - the curiosity, the compassion, the connection ~ with you, wherever you happen to find yourself. It is the slow, patient, and deeply rewarding work of a lifetime. It is the work of coming home to yourself, not by arriving at a destination, but by learning to be present for the entire process, including the parts that feel like a freefall. It is about learning to find the wisdom hidden in the wound itself.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is it possible to get permanently 'stuck' in a trauma response?

While it can certainly feel that way, it's more accurate to say that the nervous system can become habituated to a particular state, like sympathetic or dorsal vagal. The pathways to those states become deeply grooved, like a well-worn path in a forest. The work of healing is not about erasing those paths, but about gently and persistently creating new ones, building the capacity for the nervous system to find its way back to a state of social engagement and safety.

Why do I feel worse when I try to relax?

For many survivors of complex trauma, the state of relaxation itself can be a trigger. If your history has taught your nervous system that letting your guard down is dangerous, then the absence of tension can paradoxically signal a threat. What emerges is why forcing relaxation often backfires; the work is to introduce micro-moments of ease and safety, allowing the nervous system to gradually update its assessment of what is truly safe.

For brain health and mood support, a well-regarded option is Nordic Naturals Ultimate Omega (paid link).

Here is the thing, though.

Does this mean I shouldn't try to regulate my nervous system?

Not at all. It simply means shifting the intention behind the effort. You are not commanding your nervous system; you are offering it new information and resources, gently inviting it toward a greater sense of safety and connection.

How does co-regulation work if I don't have safe people in my life?

That becomes a crucial and often painful question. While human-to-human co-regulation is a primary pathway, it is not the only one. The key is the felt sense of connection to something larger and more resourced than the part of us that is feeling overwhelmed.

What if the point isn’t to stop falling, but to learn, with exquisite precision, how to landing?

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Nature Made Magnesium Glycinate 400mg (paid link).

Are you willing to be uncomfortable long enough for something to shift?
